---
title: "capstone-data-cleaning"
format: html
editor: visual
---

```{r setup/install, include=FALSE}
packages <- c(
  "tidyverse", "broom", "janitor", "skimr", 
  "tinytex", "writexl")

installed_packages <- packages %in% rownames(installed.packages())

if (any(installed_packages == FALSE)) {
  install.packages(packages[!installed_packages])
}

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
```

```{r import and look at data}
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(janitor)
library(skimr)
library(tinytex)
library(writexl)
library(stringr)

dat <- read_csv("data/PBL_DA_SP25_CAPSTONE_DATA.csv")
```

```{r look at col names}
names(dat)
```

```{r rows with na data}
rows_with_na <- dat[!complete.cases(dat), ]

# print the rows containing NA values
print(rows_with_na)
```

```{r clean p1}
dat_clean <- dat |> 
  clean_names() |> 
  drop_na()

names(dat_clean)

# glimpse
glimpse(dat_clean)
```

```{r clean p2}
dat_clean |> skim()
```

```{r clean audience_segment (combined)}
# clean up the delimiters and hierarchy in the audience column all at once

dat_clean <- dat_clean |> 
  mutate(
    # fix delimiters
    # 1. fixing the common encoding error by replacing the corrupted 'Â»' with a clean delimiter '>'
    audience_segment = str_replace_all(audience_segment, "Â»", ">"),
    
    # 2. replacing the remaining double-chevron ' »' (which sometimes has a hidden space) with a clean delimiter '>'
    audience_segment = str_replace_all(audience_segment, " »", ">"), 
    
    # 3. removing the unnecessary '<bb>' tag/separator entirely
    audience_segment = str_replace_all(audience_segment, "<bb>", ""),
    
    # fix hierarchy
    # using "-" instead of ">", fix
    # this first one fixes the "Mobile - US>" problem specifically
    audience_segment = str_replace_all(audience_segment, "Mobile - US>", "Mobile>US>"),
    
    # this one is a catch-all for any other " - " we found
    audience_segment = str_replace_all(audience_segment, " - ", ">"),
    
    # fix spacing
    # normalize spacing around the cleaned delimiters to remove all adjacent whitespace
    # 1. remove all spaces adjacent to the delimiter. 
    #    this is the core step that makes ' a > b ' or 'a> b' become 'a>b', 
    #    resulting in the clean 'level1>level2>level3' format.
    audience_segment = stringr::str_replace_all(audience_segment, "\\s*\\>\\s*", ">"),
    
    # 2. final cleanup: removing any leading or trailing spaces from the whole string
    audience_segment = stringr::str_trim(audience_segment),
    
    # 3. squishing any instances of multiple spaces left in the values themselves (e.g. 'united  states')
    audience_segment = stringr::str_squish(audience_segment) 
  )

# uncomment to check
# head(dat_clean)
```

```{r manual location looking}
# GOAL: find what "locations" are normal, as in, follow the city, state format
# find what locations do not follow the standardized format, and could be multiple cities, or city1-city2 format/DMA format
# manually verify with google maps, then check gemini

# "Orlando-Daytona Beach, Florida"
# "Grand Rapids-Kalamazoo, Michigan"  
# "Champaign & Springfield-Decatur,Illinois" 
# "Ft. Myers-Naples, Florida"  
# "Miami-Ft. Lauderdale, Florida"  
# "Tampa-St Petersburg (Sarasota), Florida"  
# "West Palm Beach-Ft. Pierce, Florida"  
# "Flint-Saginaw-Bay City, Michigan"    
# "Tallahassee, Florida-Thomasville, Georgia"
# "Tri-Cities, Tennessee-Virginia"  
# "South Bend-Elkhart, Indiana"
# "Mobile, Alabama-Pensacola, Florida"   
# "Huntsville-Decatur (Florence), Alabama" 
# "Peoria-Bloomington, Illinois"   
# "Hattiesburg-Laurel, Mississippi" 
# "Little Rock-Pine Bluff, Arkansas"     
 
```

```{r split and clean location}
# start with the cleaned data
dat_clean_sep_state <- dat_clean |>
  # step 1: initial split of location into city_raw and state_raw
  separate(
    col = location,
    into = c("city_raw", "state_raw"), # use temporary names
    sep = ",",                         # split by comma
    remove = FALSE,                    # keep original location column
    fill = "right",                    # handle rows with no comma
    extra = "merge"                    # handle rows with multiple commas
  ) |>
  # step 2: trim whitespace from the raw split columns
  mutate(
    city_raw = str_trim(city_raw),
    state_raw = str_trim(state_raw)
  ) |>
  # step 3: manually correct the few specific messy state strings
  mutate(
    state = case_when(
      state_raw == "Florida-Thomasville, Georgia" ~ "Florida",
      state_raw == "Alabama-Pensacola, Florida" ~ "Alabama",
      state_raw == "Tennessee-Virginia" ~ "Tennessee",
      TRUE ~ state_raw
    )
  ) |>
  # step 4: extract primary city name, handling "tri-cities" specifically
  mutate(
    # split city_raw by hyphen or ampersand (needed for most dmas)
    city_parts = stringr::str_split(city_raw, pattern = "\\s*[-&]\\s*"),

    # extract the first part, but use case_when for the "tri-cities" exception
    city_primary = case_when(
      # specifically handle "tri-cities" before applying the general rule
      startsWith(city_raw, "Tri-Cities") ~ "Tri-Cities",
      # default rule: extract the first part from the split list
      TRUE ~ sapply(city_parts, function(parts) parts[1])
    ),

    # clean up the extracted primary city
    city_primary = str_remove(city_primary, ",$"),
    city_primary = str_trim(city_primary),
    state = str_trim(state)
  ) |>
  # step 5: select final columns and remove temporary ones
  select(location, city = city_raw, state, city_primary, everything(), -city_parts) # remove city_parts here
```

```{r split and clean location - manually check}
# manually check cities, states, etc
# unique cities (original raw city/dma names)
unique_cities <- dat_clean_sep_state |>
  pull(city) |>  # extract the column as a vector
  unique() |>   # get only unique values
  sort()        # sort alphabetically

print("--- Unique Raw Cities (Alphabetical) ---")
print(unique_cities)

# unique primary cities (cleaned single city names)
unique_cities_primary <- dat_clean_sep_state |>
  pull(city_primary) |>
  unique() |>
  sort()

print("--- Unique Primary Cities (Alphabetical) ---")
print(unique_cities_primary)

# unique states (cleaned state names)
unique_states <- dat_clean_sep_state |>
  pull(state) |>
  unique() |>
  sort()

print("--- Unique States (Alphabetical) ---")
print(unique_states)

```

```{r fix cpg audience segments}
# # check the resulting rows, i manually fixed them in excel
# this brings that manual fix into the r script to make it reproducible

dat_clean_sep_state <- dat_clean_sep_state |> 
  mutate(
    # replace this line with whatever logic you used in excel
    # e.g., if you replaced "CPG" with "CPG>General", it would be:
    audience_segment = str_replace_all(audience_segment, "CPG", "CPG>General")
  )

# # check the resulting rows
# cpg_rows <- dat_clean_sep_state |>
#  filter(stringr::str_detect(audience_segment, "CPG"))
# print(cpg_rows)
```

```{r kpis}
# # calculate kpis for each row/campaign

dat_kpis <- dat_clean_sep_state |>
  mutate(
    # ctr: clicks / impressions
    ctr = ifelse(impressions > 0, clicks / impressions, 0),

    # conversion rate (cr): total conversions / clicks
    conv_rate = ifelse(clicks > 0, total_conversions / clicks, 0),
    
    # cpa (cost per acquisition): gross cost / total conversions
    # use na_real_ if conversions are zero, as cpa is meaningless/infinite in that case
    # na_real_ just refers to doubles/values with decimal points
    cpa = ifelse(total_conversions > 0, gross_cost / total_conversions, NA_real_),

    # cpc: gross cost / clicks
    cpc = ifelse(clicks > 0, gross_cost / clicks, NA_real_), 
    
    # eCPM: (gross cost / impressions) * 1000
    eCPM = ifelse(impressions > 0, (gross_cost / impressions) * 1000, 0),
    
    
    # viewability rate: viewable impressions / measurable impressions
    view_rate = ifelse(measurable_impressions > 0, viewable_impressions / measurable_impressions, 0),
    
    # vCPM: (gross cost / viewable impressions) * 1000
    # use na_real_ if viewable impressions are zero
    vCPM = ifelse(viewable_impressions > 0, (gross_cost / viewable_impressions) * 1000, NA_real_)
  )

# # check the new kpi columns
head(dat_kpis |> 
       select(impressions, clicks, total_conversions, gross_cost, ctr, cpa, view_rate))
```

### BUG: Some conversion rates are above 100%, or 1.00

```{r view conversion rates above 1.00}
# we found bug: basically, some conversion rates are above 100%, or 1.00
# this would imply that there are more conversions than clicks, which cannot be a thing
impossible_cr_rows <- dat_kpis |>
  filter(conv_rate > 1) |>
  select(impressions, clicks, total_conversions, gross_cost, conv_rate, location, audience_segment, app_url)

# print("--- Rows with Conversion Rate > 100% ---")
# print(impossible_cr_rows)
```

### Capped conversion rates at 100%, or 1.00

```{r cap conversion_rates at 1}
# tl;dr: cap conversion rates at 100%, if they are over 100%

# find rows where calculated conversion rate is > 1
unrealistic_conv_rate_rows <- which(dat_kpis$conv_rate > 1)

# print how many rows are affected
# print(paste("Number of rows with conv_rate > 100%:", length(unrealistic_conv_rate_rows)))

# cap the conversion rate at 1 (100%) for these rows
# note: this doesn't fix the underlying issue but prevents impossible averages
if (length(unrealistic_conv_rate_rows) > 0) {
  dat_kpis$conv_rate[unrealistic_conv_rate_rows] <- 1
  # print("Capped conv_rate at 1 for affected rows")
}

# calculated from the (potentially inflated) conversion count
```

```{r SET DATA AS CSV AND XLSX}
# dat_clean_sep_state |>
#   write_xlsx("data/dat_clean_sep_state.xlsx")
# 
# dat_clean_sep_state |>
#   write_csv("data/dat_clean_sep_state.csv")

dat_kpis |> 
  write_xlsx("data/dat_kpis.xlsx")

dat_kpis |> 
  write_csv("data/dat_kpis.csv")
```
